item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is suspected that this instance was the original vector for SCP-2009-01, although it is unknown whether it was the original instance of SCP-2009-01.

Should the first instance of "-01" be "-02"? After all, 2009-01 refers to the instances of the Hoang clones, while 2009-02 are the spores that they spread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instances of SCP-2009-01 are genetically identical, and prior to mutation, display no unusual physiology.

The process described in the following paragraph does not seem to fit the scientific definition of "mutation", unless there's a genetic change that's not mentioned. Perhaps "metamorphosis" would be a better term here? Also the first comma should be moved to between "and" and "prior".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree it's pretty scary, but I ought to burst your bubble and say that it's a spore, presumably a fungus, not a virus. it could potentially be viral because of the editing of DNA, but that's probably part of the whole anomalous thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But i will be really interesting if you mention some of the research about 2 or more Thomases interact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the duration of testing, the instance of SCP-2009-01 is to be a BSL-4-compliant containment cell kept at 10° C.

Needs a little fix there, unless Thomas Hoangs can also transform into containment cells.

item.title: Re: BAM! FAST-PACED LICENSING ACTION!
item.content:encoded: Deryck Chan, forever Thomas Hoang on this website. Maybe he could have been the original vector?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, how would you react if you saw your doppelganger?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if two of the Thomases interacted? Would anything weird happen, or would they just be really confused, or what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-2009-01 is the designation for all instances of an anomalous male humanoid capable of asexual reproduction by means of spores."

+1, it's so amusing when these "inscrutable aberrations of nature" can simply be cornered in one sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it's always hit and miss to class an object as keter because "there might be more out there", especially when all the foundation has to do is use its many resources to locate anyone using the name Thomas Huang.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good job at coming up with a novel "fungal growth" concept. I like it a lot, and the execution of the D's confusion over gender changing is really excellent and tasteful without being tacked-on for effect.

Well composed over all, with the right amount of personal identity eeriness for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting spin on an infection skip. Simple to understand, to the point and car-ree-peh.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think he's referring to SCP-1131. It's similar, but the two are distinct enough to each be interesting in their own way, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The skip was a tad bit hilarious and also horrifying (can't think up exact proportions) The second image really freaked me out when combined with the content. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
;ksadjgak Cordyceps akjg;eahyer[;iohgsk;adhj

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-998120-W, a black female, four days after exposure to SCP-2009-02. Interview took place in a darkened BSL-4-compliant containment cell.

Not sure if the Foundation would call a person "black". Maybe African American? African? IDK.

are airborne and may infect humans through the skin or respiratory system.

Skin infections with fungi are exceedingly rare. The skin is dry, salty and constantly flaking off. None of that is conducive to fungal colonization. The respiratory vector is much more sensible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A good humanoid/paracetic infection SCiP?! Impossible, I say! But wait, everything checks out here! I can't find anything wrong with this! I'm-… I'm liking this!NOOoOOOO_ *explodes into an upvote*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you point me to it? I haven't had any luck finding it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah! Fungal-based SCP! Woohoo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: downvoted due to being really similar to that SCP that is a mosquito which does exactly the same thing as this. If that didn't exist I would have upvoted. Sorry!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was intentional. I also believe that it's grammatically correct (use of had both in sense of "was obligated to" and as a marker of the past perfect) although I could be wrong. If I am, then just chalk it up to being an interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My mother had had to work late and when she got in,

You might wanna fix that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should have called this Fungidentity Crisis

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said above, if the Foundation was certain that they had the only Thomas Hoang spores safely contained, it would be Euclid. Maybe even Safe. But they aren't, and if an uncontained instance is exposed to the right conditions in, say, Miami, it will be a huge shitshow, and nearly impossible to contain.

This seems to be a point of confusion for people, so I'll modify the containment procedures to make it clear that they're still searching for uncontained instances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, this is cool. Why Keter though? Unless the spores are adept at getting out through cracks, I don't think this guy(s) would be more than Euclid. I mean, he doesn't even seek out the conditions that make him spore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tried to imply that the Foundation was unsure whether or not they had the only extant samples of Thomas Hoang, or if there were others in the wild. They got lucky when they captured the initial one, as the city was a relatively small one. If even a single Thomas Hoang "matures" in a major metropolitan area, it could cause millions of deaths Thomas Hoangs.

So, while it's easy enough to contain (although still dangerous as fuck), the possibility of there could be other instances of Mr. Hoang out there and the potential for destruction that they hold bumps it up to Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thomas Hoang is my cousin!

I enjoyed it thoroughly. An interesting way to do the whole fungal assimilation/zombie thing that's better than most that I've seen.

Little bit curious as to why this is Keter, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Odd!

Love an odd.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm assuming this was inspired by what the Cordyceps fungus does to insects. Very nicely done. The idea of the growth causing pain when the host tried to communicate was confusing, but it looks like that's been edited out. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uhm, yeah, that's better. Headcanon accepted + upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The uniform that the D-Class would have been wearing wouldn't have fit due to significant differences in height, weight, frame, etc. Probably they let him wear the shirt for the picture because why not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does it changes their clothes too? Because I can't really picture a Class D wearing a shirt. And I have a shirt just like that.

item.title: Re: BAM! FAST-PACED LICENSING ACTION!
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I had considered blackboxing it. But given that there isn't a "real" Thomas Hoang (in the Foundationverse; apologies to any IRL Thomas Hoangs I've offended), blackboxing would be kind of superflous.

You could say the same thing about blackboxing dates, times, etc. but those have potentially damaging information. Knowing that the mold monster thinks its name is Thomas Hoang won't really change anything.

item.title: Re: BAM! FAST-PACED LICENSING ACTION!
item.content:encoded:
Nicely done with the creative commons images used.

I kind of expected the guy's name to be blocked out, since that seems to be the way things normally go. Anyways, upvoted. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked it!

The whole 55kg of the virus is terrifying after having just read this.

item.title: BAM! FAST-PACED LICENSING ACTION!
item.content:encoded:

Credit for the photograph of the spores goes to Brendon Rawlings
Credit for the other photograph, which I modified, goes to "Jelly Helm"
